questions.question: Explain the Two-Phase Locking (2PL) protocol and its phases.
keyPoints: Growing phase: transaction acquires all necessary locks
Shrinking phase: transaction releases locks
No lock acquisition allowed after first lock release
Ensures conflict serializability

questions.question: What is a distributed deadlock? How is it different from a centralized deadlock?
keyPoints: Distributed deadlock involves transactions across multiple sites
Each site may have local deadlock but overall involves circular wait across sites
Global wait-for graph needed for detection
More complex to detect and resolve than centralized deadlock

questions.question: Differentiate between Wait-Die and Wound-Wait deadlock prevention schemes.
keyPoints: Wait-Die: older waits for younger, younger aborts when needs older's lock
Wound-Wait: older wounds (aborts) younger, younger waits for older
Both prevent circular wait without deadlock detection
Wait-Die causes more aborts for older; Wound-Wait causes more aborts for younger

questions.question: What is Optimistic Concurrency Control (OCC)? When is it most suitable?
keyPoints: Transactions execute without restrictions
Validation performed at commit time
Three phases: read, validation, write
Suitable when conflict rate is low
Aborts and restarts transaction if validation fails

questions.question: Explain Timestamp-Based Concurrency Control and its advantages over locking.
keyPoints: Each transaction receives unique timestamp
Operations ordered by timestamps
No locks required, so no deadlocks
Better concurrency in low-conflict scenarios
RTS and WTS maintained for each data item

questions.question: What is the phantom problem in concurrency control? How can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Phantom: different results for same query due to insertions/deletions
Occurs when predicate-based locking not used
Caused by set-oriented operations not using item locks
Prevention: predicate locking or index locking
Not prevented by simple row-level locks

questions.question: Compare centralized and distributed lock managers.
keyPoints: Centralized: single site manages all locks - simple but single point of failure
Distributed: locks managed at each site - resilient but complex
Distributed has better scalability and fault tolerance
Distributed requires more communication for global lock status
Choice depends on system requirements

questions.question: Explain the Two-Phase Commit protocol and discuss its advantages and limitations in distributed transaction management.
keyPoints: Two phases: Prepare phase (coordinator asks participants to prepare) and Commit phase (participants commit if prepared)
Coordinator logs decisions for recovery
Ensures atomic commit across distributed sites
Limitations: blocking if coordinator fails, synchronous protocol
Blocking problem: participants can block waiting for coordinator
Suitable for distributed databases requiring atomic transactions

questions.question: Describe the basic Timestamp Ordering protocol for concurrency control. How does the Thomas Write Rule improve it?
keyPoints: Each transaction gets timestamp at start
TS(T) determines serialization order
Read rule: allow if TS(T) >= WTS (item hasn't been written by younger)
Write rule: allow if TS(T) >= RTS and TS(T) >= WTS
Thomas Write Rule: allows write if TS(T) >= WTS even if TS(T) < RTS (ignore obsolete write)
Improves concurrency by not rejecting valid out-of-order writes
Maintains serializability while allowing more parallelism

questions.question: Explain the concept of serializability in distributed transactions. How is conflict serializability different from view serializability?
keyPoints: Serializability: concurrent execution equivalent to some serial order
Conflict serializability: focuses on read-write and write-read conflicts
View serializability: considers meaning of reads (initial reads, blind writes)
Conflict serializability is easier to check (precedence graph)
View serializability is more general but NP-complete to check
Distributed transactions must ensure global serializability
Local serializability not sufficient - need global ordering

questions.question: Discuss various deadlock detection and prevention techniques in distributed database systems.
keyPoints: Detection: wait-for graphs - local and global graphs, cycle = deadlock
Prevention: order resources globally, no circular wait
Wait-Die: older waits, younger aborts
Wound-Wait: older wounds younger, younger waits
Timeout-based: simple but can cause false aborts
Hierarchical: reduces complexity of global detection
Choice depends on system characteristics and requirements
